participants.reasoning: The extracted participant description matches the official criteria and sample details, with only minor paraphrasing of exclusions and the same overall female, premenopausal TMD pain population and analysis sample size.
participants.extracted: Women with temporomandibular disorder (TMD) pain were recruited from the University of Washington Orofacial Pain Clinic and by advertising between October 2005 and June 2009. Eligibility required female sex, age 18 to 45 years, a Research Diagnostic Criteria/Temporomandibular Disorders (RDC/TMD) Axis I pain diagnosis confirmed by an oral medicine specialist, premenopausal status, characteristic pain intensity of at least 3 on a 0 to 10 scale, and ability to communicate in English. Key exclusions included lack of a menstrual cycle, pregnancy, lactation, or plans to become pregnant within 7 months, unwillingness to take continuous oral contraceptives, need for further diagnostic evaluation of facial pain, and major medical or psychiatric conditions that would interfere with participation. For safety, women assigned to continuous oral contraceptive therapy also underwent gynecologic screening and were excluded for contraindications such as thromboembolic disease, cerebrovascular or coronary artery disease, certain cancers or liver disease, severe or atypical headaches, smoking at age 35 or older, interacting medications, abnormal pelvic or cervical findings, unexplained uterine bleeding, or lack of a current mammogram if age 40 or older. Of 191 randomized women, 147 were included in the analysis sample.

Exclusion criteria:lacking menstrual cycle, pregnant, lactating, planning to become pregnant in next 7 months, unwilling to take a continuous oral contraceptive, need for further diagnostic evaluation of fa

interventions.reasoning: The extracted interventions match the official ones, with detailed descriptions corresponding to self-management therapy, targeted self-management therapy, and continuous oral contraceptive therapy.
interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to one of three interventions. Self-management training (SMT) and targeted self-management training (TSMT) were dental hygienist-delivered, manual-based cognitive-behavioral/self-management programs consisting of two 1.5-hour in-person sessions and six brief telephone calls over about 6 months, plus individualized self-care planning, education about TMD, relaxation and stress management, jaw-posture and habit modification, coping skills, and relapse prevention; TSMT included all SMT components plus education about menstrual-cycle-related symptom changes, self-monitoring of pain across the menstrual cycle, and planning for anticipated pain increases at specific cycle phases, with session timing tailored to the menstrual cycle. The comparison intervention was continuous oral contraceptive therapy (COCT), in which women received ethinyl estradiol 20 mcg plus levonorgestrel 100 mcg with continuous use for 6 months to avoid hormone withdrawal, along with gynecologic evaluation, medication instructions, pregnancy testing, and adverse-event monitoring.
interventions.official:
Self‐management therapy
Targeted self‐management therapy
Continuous oral contraceptive therapy

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include several measures not listed in the official review outcomes, and the official list includes GCPS and adverse effects in a different way, so the outcome reporting is not fully consistent.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: characteristic pain intensity, measured using the Graded Chronic Pain Scale (GCPS) as the average of current facial pain, average facial pain, and worst facial pain over the past month on 0–10 ratings; assessed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Secondary outcomes: activity interference, measured with the GCPS as the average of 0–10 ratings of facial pain interference with daily activities, work and housework, and recreational/social/family activities; assessed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Clinically meaningful improvement in pain, defined a priori as a reduction in characteristic pain intensity from baseline of at least 30% (clinically meaningful) and at least 50% (substantial); assessed at 6 months and 12 months. Pain quality, measured with the Short Form McGill Pain Questionnaire (SF MPQ) sensory and affective subscales; assessed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Depression, measured with the 21-item Beck Depression Inventory (BDI); assessed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Treatment helpfulness, measured at 6 months using 0–10 ratings of overall improvement in facial pain, overall satisfaction with treatment, and helpfulness of specific treatment components (including, for TSMT, planning for menstrual cycle-related pain increases). Process measures: pain beliefs assessed with the Survey of Pain Attitudes (SOPA) Disability, Harm, and Control subscales; self-efficacy assessed with the TMD Self-Efficacy Scale; pain catastrophizing assessed with the Coping Strategies Questionnaire catastrophizing scale; and perceived effectiveness of pain coping strategies assessed with CSQ ratings of ability to control and decrease pain. These process measures were assessed at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Adverse events were monitored throughout treatment, including medication-related adverse events in the COCT group and treatment-related adverse events in the self-management groups.
